rationale: We consolidate our gains not by reckless advance, but by turning every meter of captured ground into a tomb for the enemy—let them bleed trying to take it back.
Rotating fresh brigades into the rear zones maintains pressure and keeps Tethys guessing—our restraint is tactical, not strategic.
A visible mobilization along the secondary front reminds all who doubt our resolve that Novaris does not negotiate from weakness.
If we are to act, we must see clearly—our priority is penetrating Tethys’ command structure and energy security planning before any decision point.
With heightened tensions, foreign assets will swarm us; we must clean house now before they exploit our vulnerabilities.
Stealing Tethys’ logistical and reserve deployment data gives us asymmetric leverage—knowledge is the decisive terrain.
We cannot afford to lose 30% of energy revenue, but we can strangle Tethys’ luxury imports to force concessions without triggering full rupture.
Targeted halt on rare earth exports pressures their tech sector and defense industry while leaving diplomatic off-ramps.
Quiet devaluation of our rouble reduces dependency on Tethyan transit fees by making alternative overland routes more viable long-term.
The enemy’s overextension is our opening—targeting forward logistics nodes will bleed their momentum and prove we won’t just dig in and die.
Shifting armored reserves to secondary axes keeps Novaris guessing and prevents them from massing for another breakthrough.
A high-visibility armored parade near the capital will rally the people and signal resolve—morale is a weapon too.
We have confirmation of Novaris’s supply strain—now is the time to hit their rail hubs and fuel depots covertly and deepen their operational paralysis.
Disrupting their military logistics networks will amplify physical sabotage and create cascading delays they can’t explain publicly.
A well-planted incident in a neutral zone could fracture Novaris’s domestic support and force them to divert resources inward.
While the guns pause, we must probe Novaris’s true red lines—this crisis won’t end on the battlefield alone.
Offering a neutral third party to facilitate talks positions us as responsible and could expose Novaris as the intransigent party.
Novaris depends on Tethys for 30% of its energy revenue—we cut that flow, and we cut their war chest at its source.
Secondary pressure on dual-use imports will strangle their defense industry’s ability to replace lost equipment.
